model_narrative: We plan which warehouses to open and which warehouse serves each customer region. Each candidate warehouse can be opened at a fixed cost and has a limited capacity. Each region has a demand and must be served, and serving a region from a given warehouse incurs a transportation cost that depends on the pair. We decide which warehouses to open and how to assign regions to warehouses so that the total cost, made up of fixed opening costs and transportation costs, is as small as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the candidate warehouses that may be opened to serve regions

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the customer regions, each of which must be served by exactly one warehouse

params.name: f
params.doc: the fixed cost charged for opening each warehouse, in dollars

params.name: c
params.doc: the capacity of each warehouse, expressed as the maximum total region demand it can serve, in demand units

params.name: d
params.doc: the demand of each region, in demand units

params.name: t
params.doc: the transportation cost of serving a region from a warehouse, in dollars; indexed with the region first and the warehouse second

vars.name: totcost
vars.doc: the total cost, equal to fixed cost plus transportation cost, in dollars

vars.name: fcost
vars.doc: the total fixed cost of all opened warehouses, in dollars

vars.name: tcost
vars.doc: the total transportation cost of all serving assignments, in dollars

vars.name: ow
vars.doc: 1 if the warehouse is opened, 0 otherwise

vars.name: oa
vars.doc: 1 if the warehouse serves the region, 0 otherwise; indexed with the warehouse first and the region second

constraints.name: totcost
constraints.intent: The total cost must be the sum of the two cost components the plan incurs. Set the total cost equal to the fixed cost plus the transportation cost.

constraints.name: fcost
constraints.intent: The total fixed cost comes from opening warehouses. Set the fixed cost equal to the sum over all warehouses of each warehouse's opening cost counted only when that warehouse is opened.

constraints.name: tcost
constraints.intent: The total transportation cost comes from how regions are served. Set the transportation cost equal to the sum over every warehouse and region pair of the cost of serving that region from that warehouse counted only when that warehouse serves that region.

constraints.name: d[j]
constraints.intent: Each warehouse can only handle so much demand. For each warehouse, the total demand of the regions it serves must not exceed that warehouse's capacity.

constraints.name: oa[i,
constraints.intent: Every region must be served, and by a single warehouse. For each region, exactly one warehouse serves it.

constraints.name: ow[i]
constraints.intent: A region can only be served by a warehouse that is open. For each warehouse and region pair, the warehouse must be open whenever it serves that region.

query: The board wants a real foothold in the central corridor, not a token presence — there's a proposal to push a meaningful chunk of volume through warehouse 3, on the order of 90 units of regional demand routed via that site. Fold that into the plan and tell me where total cost lands.
expected_pyomo: model.w3_corridor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.d[j] * model.oa['w3', j] for j in model.j) >= 90)

query: Risk is pitching a resilience floor for the network — they want at least three of our warehouses live at any time so no single closure strands a region. Let's try that: require three or more sites open and tell me the resulting total cost.
expected_pyomo: model.min_open = Constraint(expr=sum(model.ow[i] for i in model.i) >= 3)

query: Account management is pushing a single-source diversification rule: regions 1 and 2 are two of our biggest contracts and they don't want both of them riding on the same warehouse, so one site shouldn't carry both of them at once. We're evaluating that — apply it and tell me what the network ends up costing.

query: Our open warehouses look maxed out — they're running with barely any slack, which leaves us no cushion for a demand spike or an outage. Shouldn't every site we operate hold back some buffer, say keep the demand it serves under about seventy percent of its storage capacity, rather than packing them to the ceiling? What's the tradeoff keeping the plan from spreading the load out that way?

query: It bugs me that warehouse 2 has the bigger footprint yet ends up carrying less of the demand than warehouse 1 in this plan. If w2 is the larger site, shouldn't it shoulder the heavier load — say at least thirty units more of regional demand than w1 does — instead of leaving that capacity underused? What's the tradeoff pulling the plan the other way?
expected_pyomo: model.w2_carries_more = Constraint(expr=sum(model.d[j] * model.oa['w2', j] for j in model.j) >= sum(model.d[j] * model.oa['w1', j] for j in model.j) + 30)

query: We're paying fixed costs on two separate warehouses. Couldn't we just consolidate everything into a single site and save all that overhead? Walk me through what's keeping the plan from collapsing down to one warehouse.
expected_pyomo: model.single_site = Constraint(expr=sum(model.ow[i] for i in model.i) <= 1)
